title: practical english usage - Part 141
content:
178  reflexive       pronouns:      myself,       etc 1 wish I had a room of my own.

The  word own is used in a similar structure (►   552).

He's a cousin of the Queen.  She's a friend of my father.

sometimes used when talking about relationships.

The  structure has a variant in which  a noun does not have possessive 's: this is grammar •  179   reciprocal pronouns: each other and one another Each other is more common than one another, especially in an informal style.

Anna and I write to each other / one another every week.

Each other and one another mean the same.

on  e  anothe       r   _____ _________ 179 reciprocal       pronouns:      each     other      and For more about structures like The door opened and His book's selling well, ►    9.

Hurry! (     Hurry yourself.) not I feel strange. (     I feel myself strange.) no t Try to concentrate. (     Try to concentrate yourself.) not His book's selling well. (    H is book's selling itself well.) not Suddenly the door opened. (     Suddenly the door opened itself.) Some other verbs which  do not normally have reflexive pronouns: So does he shave himself, or not?

The barber shaves all the men in the town who don't shave themselves.

She's old enough to dress herself now.

action.

However, reflexives can be used if it is necessary to make it clear who  does the not    o Do you shave on Sundays? (     D     you shave yourself on Sundays?) languages but not in English.

Certain verbs (e.g. wash, dress, shave) have reflexive pronouns in some 10   reflexives not used The children have both got their own rooms.

I always wash my own clothes. (     .. .myself's clothes.) There are no possessive reflexives. Instead, we  use my  own, your own, etc.

They  talk to each other a lot. (Each of them talks to the other.) They talk to themselves a lot. (Each of them talks to him/herself.) Note the difference between -selves and each other / one another (►   179).

8                   and each           /  one -selves                other            another 'Do you need help? 'No, thanks. I can do it by myself' I often like to spend time by myself.

By myself/yourself etc means 'alone, without company’  or 'without help’ .

There will be four of us at dinner: Robert, Alison, Jenny and myself.

phrases.

Reflexives can also be used instead of personal pronouns in co-ordinated noun or Everybody was early except myself (   ... except me.) or These shoes are designed for heavy runners like yourself. (   ... like you.) PronounsSection  14 grammar «180     somebody, someone, anybody, anyone, etc 1 don 't want to go anywhere too expensive.

- Let's go somewhere nice for dinner.

If you need something/anything, just shout.

- Can I get you something to drink?

Did anybody telephone?

- There's somebody at the door.

any(*  161   for details). Compare: somewhere and anywhere, etc are the same as the differences between some and The  differences between somebody and anybody, something and anything, 2 some-      and   any- English.

common in writing; the -body forms are more frequent in speech in British anyone, everybody and everyone, or nobody and no one. The  -one forms are more There is no significant difference between somebody and someone, anybody and 1  -body      and   -one phrases when it is not necessary, or not possible, to be more precise.

These indefinite pronouns and adverbials replace more definite noun/adverbial 180 somebody,           someone,          anybody,         anyone,        etc They met in 1992 and married in 1994.

where the verb itself makes the meaning clear.

We   do not normally use each other / one another after words like meet or marry, 5 words     used    without      each      other      I one     another (I'm interested in hers; she’ s interested in mine.) My   girlfriend and I are both very interested in each other's work.

mine; she's interested in hers.) My   girlfriend and I are both very interested in our work. (I'm interested in Compare: There is a similar difference between possessives and each other / one another.
